data: cluster_001
Hawaii doctor accused of trying to kill his wife convicted of attempted manslaughter
wsvn.com

A Hawaii anesthesiologist, Gerhardt Konig, has been convicted of attempted manslaughter for trying to kill his wife during a hike last year. The conviction carries a sentence of up to 20 years in prison, with sentencing set for August 13. Konig's attorney plans to appeal the verdict, which was based on the jury's finding that Konig acted under extreme mental or emotional disturbance due to his wife's relationship with a coworker.
cluster_002
Former Fort Bragg employee charged with leaking classified military information to journalist
foxnews.com

A former Fort Bragg employee, Courtney P. Williams, 40, has been arrested and charged with unlawfully transmitting national defense information to a journalist. Williams, who held a Top Secret/Sensitive Compartmented Information clearance, allegedly leaked classified military tactics and information to the journalist over several years. The leaked information, which included classified materials at the "SECRET" level, could pose serious risks to military personnel and operations, according to authorities.
cluster_003
NYT Pips hints, answers for April 9, 2026
mashable.com

The New York Times' game Pips, released in August 2025, is a unique spin on dominoes with color-coded conditions. The game offers hints and answers for each difficulty level, including easy, medium, and hard, with specific conditions such as "Equal", "Less Than", and "Greater Than". The article provides piecemeal answers to help players navigate through each difficulty level.
cluster_004
Jim Whittaker, first American climber to scale Mt. Everest, dies at 97
latimes.com

Jim Whittaker, the first American to summit Mt. Everest, has died at the age of 97. He reached the summit on May 1, 1963, with Sherpa Nawang Gombu, and his achievement made him a hero and celebrity. Whittaker died at his home in Port Townsend, Washington, leaving behind a legacy as an adventurer and mountaineer.
cluster_005
‘The Housemaid’ author Frieda McFadden reveals true identity after living double life for 23 years
pagesix.com

Author Frieda McFadden, known for her book "The Housemaid", has revealed her true identity as Sara Cohen, a 45-year-old New York-born doctor. Cohen had kept her writing career a secret for 23 years, using a pseudonym and disguise to maintain her privacy. She has now scaled back her medical work and is speaking publicly about her dual life as a doctor and author.
